betterquesting:10.desc:8:
The §3Firebox§r and the §3Heating Oven§r are machines capable of producing heat (§eTU§r) from solid fuels for any machines that require heat to operate.

Both machines are similar in operation. In order for them to transfer heat, another machine for receiving the heat must be placed on top, such that the copper heat plate on the receiving machine sits on top of the copper heat plate of the heat producing machine.

Once constructed, these machines can be filled with solid fuel, which will burn until either the fuel runs out, or the heat buffer is filled. No fuel will be consumed if its buffer is full — as such, both machines are fairly efficient at conserving fuel.

The primary difference in these two machines lie in their speed: the firebox burns fuel much slower at full efficiency, whilst the heating oven produces five time the heat (but eight times faster). Despite its inefficiency, the heating oven is often preferred for its much higher heat outputs and heat capacity. However, it cannot heat a stirling engine, as its base output (500 TU/t) will automatically over-speed the engine; the heavy stirling engine does not have this issue.

Additionally, the heating oven can receive heat from other heat producing machines placed underneath it: multiple can be stacked for heat-hungry machines like the crucible. The heat received, however, is halved when transferring to the machine above.
